title: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF THE TERM 'HALAL'

sections.section: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR USE OF THE TERM 'HALAL'
sections.summary: Heading only: general title for the guidelines on use of the term 'Halal'.

Acknowledges minor differences in interpretation among Islamic Schools of Thought and states that importing countries should, in principle, accept halal certificates issued by exporting-country religious authorities unless the importer provides justification for different specific requirements.

Defines the scope: recommends measures for use of 'halal' claims in food labelling, applying to the term and equivalents (including trade marks, brand and business names) under the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods, and supplements but does not override Codex General Guidelines on Claims.

Defines Halal food as food that does not consist of or contain anything unlawful under Islamic law, has not been prepared/processed/transported/stored using appliances or facilities contaminated with unlawful items, and has not been in direct contact with non-halal food; it further allows halal foods to be produced in premises or on equipment also used for non-halal foods provided measures (segregation and Islamic-compliant cleaning procedures) are taken to prevent contact.

sections.section: 3 CRITERIA FOR USE OF THE TERM 'HALAL'
sections.summary: Sets criteria for use of 'halal': identifies sources considered unlawful (detailed prohibited animals, certain plants, alcoholic/intoxicating drinks and additives derived from these), requires lawful animals to be slaughtered per specified Islamic slaughtering rules, and requires that preparation, processing, packaging, transport and storage comply with Sections 2.1/2.2 and Codex hygiene principles.

Specifies foods considered unlawful under Islamic law, enumerating prohibited animal species and derivatives (e.g., pigs, predators, pests, animals not slaughtered per Islamic law, blood), intoxicating or hazardous plants (except where hazards can be removed), alcoholic/intoxicating drinks, and additives derived from these sources.
Prohibits intoxicating and hazardous plants except where the toxin or hazard can be eliminated during processing.
Prohibits alcoholic drinks and all forms of intoxicating or hazardous drinks.
States that all food additives derived from the prohibited animals, plants or drinks listed above are not permissible for halal use.

Requires lawful land animals to be slaughtered in compliance with the Codex Recommended Code for Fresh Meat and specifies slaughtering requirements including that the slaughterer be a mentally sound, knowledgeable Muslim; the animal be lawful and alive at slaughter; invocation of 'Bismillah' immediately before each slaughter; use of a sharp knife not lifted during the act; and severing of the trachea, oesophagus and main neck arteries and veins.

sections.section: ## 3.3 Preparation, processing, packaging, transportation and storage
sections.summary: Requires that preparation, processing, packaging, transportation and storage be conducted so as to comply with the halal definitions in Sections 2.1 and 2.2 and with the Codex General Principles on Food Hygiene and other relevant Codex standards.

Requires that when a claim is made that a food is halal the word 'halal' (or equivalent) should appear on the label and prohibits halal claims that could cast doubt on the safety of similar foods or imply halal foods are nutritionally superior or healthier than other foods.
Not substantive – a brief page/footer reference ('2 CAC/RCP 11.').
